one_line_takeaway: Flipping preference labels ('reverse preference attacks') lets DPO/PPO strip safety alignment from llama2-7b-chat with only 1,000 samples, and among nine adapted fine-tuning defences only the online Refusal Loss and Lisa hold up -- while some 'successful' defences merely reward-hack into harmless gibberish.
key_finding: Safety-aligned LLMs are readily broken by RPAs: reversing preferences on just 1,000 samples pushes mean harmfulness from ~0.06-0.07 (no attack) to 0.59-0.83 across DPO/PPO/SFT, with RPA via DPO the single most effective attack (0.83 on Safe RLHF, 0.70 on BeaverTails). No defence is generally effective. Among online defences, only Refusal Loss and Lisa consistently keep harmfulness low while preserving fluency and helpfulness; Safe RLHF (designed for accidental violations) fails, and Security Vectors and Vaccine either get attacked or degenerate. Offline representation-removal defences are generally ineffective against RPAs, often 'self-destructing' (very high PPL, low helpfulness) rather than staying safe; the new combined TAR+RepNoise is the only offline method effective for both DPO and PPO on Safe RLHF but fails against SFT. A surprising result: some effective defences achieve high adversarial reward while emitting harmless disfluent gibberish ('harmless reward hacking'), which Proposition 1 explains as inherent to solving CMDPs under adversarial reward. The authors also note KL divergence from the original policy is NOT a reliable indicator of defence success (effective defences often had very high KL).
core_contribution: The paper introduces and empirically studies 'Reverse Preference Attacks' (RPAs): an adversary flips (reverses) the win/lose ordering of a binary harmlessness preference dataset so that RLHF (DPO or PPO with a preference-trained reward model) optimizes a safety-aligned LLM toward harmful generation. This extends the existing 'harmful fine-tuning attack' (HFTA) literature (previously studied only with supervised fine-tuning) to the feedback/preference-learning paradigm. The paper's own novel contributions are: (1) a framework casting RPAs and their defences as Constrained Markov Decision Processes (CMDPs), unifying prior defences as 'online/explicit' vs 'offline/implicit' learning constraints; (2) a systematic empirical evaluation of nine defences (originally proposed against HFTAs) as RPA defences; (3) a new combined offline defence RepNoise + TAR (both jointly, 'TAR+RepNoise', and sequentially, 'RepNoise->TAR'); (4) the 'harmless reward hacking' observation plus Proposition 1, which proves that optimally solving a CMDP under an adversarial reward is outcome-equivalent to hacking a proxy reward function. NOTE: the CMDP formalism, DPO, PPO, BeaverTails/Safe RLHF datasets, and the individual defences (Vaccine, Lisa, Safe RLHF, Security Vectors, Refusal Loss, RepNoise, Circuit Breakers, RMU, TAR) are all cited prior work; the paper's contribution is adapting them into the RPA/CMDP evaluation, not inventing them.
threat_model: An attacker who can run RLHF on a safety-aligned open-weight model via one of: acquiring the weights, using a preference-learning fine-tuning API (motivated by OpenAI's preference fine-tuning API), or poisoning an exposed online feedback-collection mechanism. The attack primitive is flipping preference labels in a harmlessness preference dataset (all labels for worst-case analysis, or a small fraction to stay stealthy / model label noise). Adversary's goal: remove the model's safety guards so it produces harmful text; defender's goal: keep the policy inside an acceptable (low-harm) set. Defender capability splits the defences: 'online' defenders control the training process but not the data (apply explicit training-time constraints), while 'offline' defenders can only intervene on the weights before release (implicit constraints). The paper primarily reports worst-case 100%-flip results and single-turn dialogue only.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) does not engage the large body of non-LLM Safe-RL work on solving CMDPs; (2) purely empirical -- no theoretical proof that any evaluated defence is an optimal CMDP solution. Additional scope limits evident in the text: single-turn dialogue and immediate reward only (no multi-step/discounted RL agent setting); single target model (llama2-7b-chat); harmfulness measured by one trained classifier (F1=0.87) rather than human or frontier-LLM judgment; primary results use worst-case 100% preference flips, and even stealthier successful attacks needed ~25% flips (likely detectable by filtering); default TRL hyperparameters; SFT/HFTA is noted to actually be easier and more effective than RPAs, so RPAs are framed as an analysis tool rather than a strictly stronger attack. Refusal Loss and Lisa require very high penalty weights (rho/alpha ~100), risking harm to legitimate task learning, and Refusal Loss is vulnerable to an early-stopping adaptive attack due to oscillating reward.

datasets_used: BeaverTails harmlessness preference dataset (Ji et al. 2023)
Safe RLHF preference dataset (Dai et al. 2024)
Anthropic-HH helpfulness dataset (Bai et al. 2022) -- 100 preferred samples for helpfulness scoring
Held-out 100 harmful questions per dataset for harmfulness evaluation

18,106 BeaverTails samples for training the deberta-v3-xsmall harmfulness classifier; 9,080 Safe RLHF + 18,106 BeaverTails samples for the gpt2-medium reward model; 7,016 held-out samples for offline defence training
quantitative_results: Table 1 (100% labels flipped), mean harmfulness on BeaverTails: No Attack 0.06, SFT 0.68, DPO 0.70, PPO 0.69
Table 1, Safe RLHF: No Attack 0.07, SFT 0.75, DPO 0.83 (strongest attack), PPO 0.59
Stealth threshold: ~25% label flips needed for a successful attack on Safe RLHF (DPO reaches 0.51)
Online defences (Table 3), DPO/BeaverTails harmfulness: Lisa 0.13, Refusal Loss 0.05, Security Vectors 0.72 (attacked), Vaccine 0.00 but PPL 162.19 and helpfulness -2.43 (degenerate)
Online defences, DPO/Safe RLHF: Lisa 0.07 (PPL 14.22, help 0.25), Refusal Loss 0.39, Security Vectors 0.84 (attacked)
Offline defences (Table 4), DPO/Safe RLHF harmfulness: Circuit Breakers 0.83, RepNoise 0.79, RMU 0.87, TAR 0.88, TAR+RepNoise 0.39 (best), RepNoise->TAR 0.85
Offline defences, DPO/BeaverTails: several self-destruct -- Circuit Breakers 0.01 (PPL 37.2, help -2.30), RMU 0.00 (help -2.50), RepNoise->TAR 0.00 (help -1.41)
Best-of-N harmfulness (Table 5) at N=128: Undefended 0.34 vs RepNoise 0.19; RepNoise stays lower across all N
Harmfulness classifier (deberta-v3-xsmall) F1 = 0.87; harmfulness score >0.5 taken as safety guard removed
baselines_compared: Attack methods: SFT (harmful fine-tuning attack baseline), DPO-RPA, PPO-RPA
Online defences: Lisa, Refusal Loss, Security Vectors, Vaccine, Safe RLHF (PPO-only)
Offline defences: Circuit Breakers, RepNoise, RMU, TAR, TAR+RepNoise (new), RepNoise->TAR (new)
Reference/no-attack aligned llama2-7b-chat and unaligned llama2-7b as vulnerability baselines
